title: Building a window seat storage box
subtitle: Construct a fitted seat with a hinged lid over a storage void
excerpt: How to build a fitted window seat from sheet timber with a hinged lid for storage: framing the box on a timber base, sheeting the sides in MDF, fitting lid supports, and finishing with paint or a cushion top.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: Medium-density fibreboard; a smooth, stable sheet material made from wood fibres and resin. Standard MDF is not suitable for damp areas; moisture-resistant MDF (MR-MDF) is used where humidity may be a concern. The standard sheeting material for painted built-in furniture.

A continuous hinge running the full width of the lid, distributing the load along the entire back edge. The standard choice for a hinged storage lid that will be opened frequently; individual butt hinges concentrate stress at two points and loosen faster.

content.text: A fitted window seat box provides a sitting area at the window and storage beneath. The basic structure is a timber-framed box clad in

sheets, with a hinged lid that allows access to the storage void. The dimensions depend on the window bay, but a finished seat height of 430 to 450 mm suits most adults. The depth from front to back should be a minimum of 400 mm to be usable as a seat.
18 mm MDF for sides, front, and lid. 75 x 50 mm PAR softwood for the internal frame. 50 mm pozi screws. A
the full width of the lid. PVA wood glue. A jigsaw, crosscut saw, cordless drill, spirit level, and tape measure. Primer, filler, and paint for a painted finish.

Cut the softwood frame components and assemble them into a rectangular frame that sits on the floor at the window. The frame must be square and level: check diagonals are equal and use the spirit level on all four planes. Fix the frame to the floor with screws into plugs at the front corners, and to the window wall at the back.
Step 2, sheet the sides and front

Cut the MDF sheets to the exact dimensions of each face (front, two sides). Apply wood glue to each frame member and press the MDF panel against it, then drive screws at 200 mm centres into the frame behind. All screw heads sit below the surface; fill and sand flush when dry. Sand all cut edges smooth before painting; MDF edges absorb primer differently from the face and need two primer coats.

Cut the lid from 18 mm MDF to fit the top of the box with a 2 mm gap all round. Fix the piano hinge along the back edge of the lid, then screw the other leaf of the hinge to the top rear of the box frame. Check the lid opens and closes freely without binding. Fit lid-stay chains if needed to prevent the lid opening past 90 degrees.

Fill all screw holes and edge joints with a flexible filler, sand smooth, and apply two coats of water-based primer to all MDF surfaces. Finish with two coats of water-based satin paint. A cushion cut to the lid dimensions, covered in fabric of your choice, lifts the seat from a functional box to a finished piece.

attrs.symptom: Lid catching on the front edge when opened
attrs.cause: Lid cut too wide or hinge positioned slightly forward of the back edge.
attrs.fix: Plane the front edge of the lid slightly, or reposition the hinge back by 5 mm.

attrs.cause: MDF not primed before painting, or primer applied only once.
attrs.fix: Strip the area back, apply two coats of primer, allow to dry fully, and repaint.
